agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) with Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling problem with significant domain implications.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request threatens to collapse two distinct bounded contexts into a tightly coupled system.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) representing the current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management)."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An item managed by the warehouse with an associated StockLevel. Its identity is shared across contexts but its meaning differs — in Context A it is a physical inventory item, in Context B it is a catalog reference on an OrderLine."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order, referencing a Product by ID and specifying a Quantity desired."},{"term":"Stock Availability","definition":"A read-only projection of StockLevel from Context A, consumed by Context B for informational or decision-making purposes. NOT the authoritative StockLevel itself."},{"term":"Reservation","definition":"A time-bounded claim on inventory that reduces available stock without yet completing a pick. This concept does NOT currently exist in either context and would need to be introduced."},{"term":"Order Blocking","definition":"The proposed mechanism to reject or hold an Order when stock is deemed insufficient. The blocking policy is a business decision, not a physical constraint."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for a Product must never go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Negative stock represents a physically impossible state — you cannot pick items that do not exist. If violated, the warehouse data is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Given any Product, its StockLevel is always >= 0 at every point in time."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Context B must NEVER directly write to or own StockLevel data. StockLevel is exclusively owned by Context A.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating aggregate ownership across bounded contexts destroys data consistency. Two writers to the same data with no single source of truth leads to corrupt inventory.","testable_assertion":"No command originating from Context B modifies StockLevel directly; all inventory mutations flow through Context A's domain services."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock availability information visible to Context B is an eventually-consistent read projection, not the authoritative StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Real-time stock is a lie in any distributed system. The moment Context B reads a value, it may already be stale due to concurrent picks, receiving, or other orders. Treating a projection as authoritative leads to overselling or false blocks.","testable_assertion":"The stock availability indicator in Context B is sourced from an async projection (event or polling) and is never used as a synchronous gate for order placement without a reservation protocol."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Blocking an order due to insufficient stock is a business POLICY, not an invariant. Backorders, partial fulfillment, and pre-orders are all legitimate business strategies.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"A VP of Sales could decide tomorrow to allow backorders. This is a configurable policy, not a law of physics. Encoding it as a hard invariant would prevent legitimate business flexibility.","testable_assertion":"The order-blocking behavior is governed by a configurable policy that can be enabled, disabled, or modified (e.g., allow backorders, allow partial fulfillment) without code changes to the core Order aggregate."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"If stock-based order blocking is implemented, it must use a Reservation pattern — a time-bounded claim on inventory — not a point-in-time stock check.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"A point-in-time check is a TOCTOU race condition. Between check and pick, stock may change. The reservation pattern is the correct domain solution, but its parameters (timeout, retry) are policy.","testable_assertion":"When an Order is accepted, a Reservation is created in Context A that decrements available stock. If the Reservation expires without fulfillment, stock is released."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Orders placed by customers currently do not check Context A. Introducing a synchronous dependency means order placement now fails if the warehouse system is down.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an availability tradeoff, not a physical constraint. But it is a critical operational decision: coupling order placement to warehouse availability degrades the customer experience.","testable_assertion":"If Context A is unavailable, Context B can still accept orders (possibly flagged for later validation) rather than failing the customer-facing operation."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an Order with OrderLines referencing Products that have sufficient stock","when":"The Order is submitted","then":"The Order is accepted and a Reservation is created in Context A for the requested quantities, reducing available stock for other orders"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine where the requested Quantity exceeds available stock","when":"The Order is submitted and the blocking policy is ENABLED","then":"The Order is rejected or held with a clear reason indicating which Product(s) have insufficient stock"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine where stock is insufficient","when":"The blocking policy is DISABLED (e.g., backorders allowed)","then":"The Order is accepted normally, and the fulfillment process handles the shortfall downstream"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The Warehouse Management system (Context A) is unavailable","when":"A customer attempts to place an Order","then":"The Order is accepted in a 'pending validation' state rather than failing, and is validated against stock when Context A recovers"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Reservation was created for an Order","when":"The Reservation expires without the Order being fulfilled (e.g., payment timeout)","then":"The reserved stock is released back to available inventory in Context A"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The sales team views an Order or Product catalog","when":"Stock availability data is displayed","then":"The indicator is clearly labeled as approximate/near-real-time and sourced from an eventually-consistent projection, not a synchronous call to Context A"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders for the last unit of a Product","when":"Both orders are submitted concurrently","then":"Exactly one order receives the reservation; the other is blocked or queued. StockLevel never goes below zero."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Two concurrent orders compete for the last unit of stock","expected_behavior":"The Reservation mechanism in Context A must serialize access. One order gets the reservation; the other is rejected or queued. StockLevel invariant (>= 0) is never violated."},{"scenario":"Stock is received (restocked) while an order is being blocked","expected_behavior":"The order should be re-evaluable. If using a reservation pattern, the customer could retry. If using an event-driven approach, a blocked order could be automatically unblocked when stock arrives."},{"scenario":"A Reservation is created but the customer abandons checkout","expected_behavior":"The Reservation must have a TTL. On expiry, stock is released. Without this, phantom reservations permanently reduce available stock."},{"scenario":"The stock availability indicator shows 'in stock' but by the time the customer submits, stock is gone","expected_behavior":"This is expected behavior in an eventually-consistent system. The order submission must handle this gracefully with a clear error message, not a system crash or silent failure."},{"scenario":"Partial stock availability — 3 units requested, 2 available","expected_behavior":"Policy decision: reject entire order, allow partial fulfillment, or split into fulfilled and backordered portions. This must be configurable, not hardcoded."},{"scenario":"Bulk/B2B order for thousands of units","expected_behavior":"Reservation of large quantities must not create a long lock. Consider whether bulk orders follow the same blocking policy or have separate business rules."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Context B catalog but has never been stocked in Context A","expected_behavior":"No StockLevel record exists. The system must handle missing stock data gracefully — either treating it as zero stock or flagging for manual review."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders prevents overselling and reduces costly cancellations/refunds. However, false blocks from stale data or overly aggressive policies directly reduce revenue. The cost of a false block (lost sale) must be weighed against the cost of an oversell (fulfillment failure + customer disappointment).","operational":"Introduces a hard runtime dependency between two previously independent systems. Warehouse system downtime now directly impacts sales throughput. Operational complexity increases significantly — monitoring, alerting, and incident response must now span both contexts. The reservation mechanism adds state that must be managed (expiry, cleanup).","user":"Sales team gets stock visibility they currently lack — a clear UX improvement. However, customers may experience order rejections they did not previously see (orders that would have been accepted and handled downstream are now blocked upfront). The 'real-time' stock indicator may create false confidence if not clearly labeled as approximate.","risk":"HIGH — This integration couples two bounded contexts that were designed to be independent. The primary risks are: (1) TOCTOU race conditions if implemented as a simple check-then-act, (2) cascading failures if Context A outages block all order placement, (3) domain model corruption if stock ownership boundaries are violated, (4) revenue loss from overly aggressive blocking policies. The 'real-time' framing is misleading and may drive incorrect architectural decisions.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"May experience new order rejections. Checkout flow becomes dependent on warehouse system availability. Positive: fewer post-order cancellations due to stock-outs."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility indicator — improves ability to advise customers. Must understand the indicator is approximate, not authoritative."},{"group":"Warehouse Operations","impact":"Now has a new consumer of stock data with real-time expectations. Reservation management adds operational overhead. Stock adjustments (corrections, cycle counts) now have immediate downstream impact on order flow."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Must build and maintain a cross-context integration (anti-corruption layer, event bus or API, reservation lifecycle management). Monitoring and incident response complexity increases."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Reduced overselling costs vs. potential revenue loss from false blocks. Net impact depends entirely on policy calibration and data freshness."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is ambiguous in critical areas: (1) 'automatically block' could mean reject, hold, or queue — these are very different domain behaviors; (2) 'real time' stock visibility is physically impossible in a distributed system and suggests a misunderstanding of the constraints; (3) no mention of what happens when Context A is unavailable; (4) no mention of reservations, backorders, or partial fulfillment — all of which are fundamental to this domain. The feature request reads like a stakeholder wish, not a specification.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 is a hard invariant","Context A owns StockLevel exclusively","Stock availability in Context B must be eventually consistent","Blocking policy must be configurable, not hardcoded","TOCTOU race condition is a real risk without reservations"],"low_confidence_areas":["What 'block' means — reject vs hold vs queue","Whether backorders should be supported","Reservation TTL and retry semantics","Behavior when Context A is unavailable","Whether partial fulfillment is in scope","How 'real-time' the stock indicator needs to be"],"assumptions":["The two bounded contexts are deployed as separate services or modules with independent data stores","There is no existing integration or shared database between Context A and Context B","The business would prefer to accept orders gracefully over hard-failing when stock data is unavailable","A reservation pattern is the correct domain solution for cross-context stock claiming"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Automatically block' is undefined. Does this mean: (A) reject the order immediately with an error, (B) accept the order in a 'held' state pending manual review, or (C) queue the order for retry when stock becomes available? These are fundamentally different domain behaviors with different UX, operational, and financial implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Operations"],"options":["(A) Hard reject — customer must retry manually","(B) Soft hold — order accepted but flagged, sales team intervenes","(C) Auto-queue — order waits for restock automatically"],"recommendation":"Option B (soft hold) is the safest default. It preserves the customer's intent while giving the sales team visibility. Hard rejects lose sales; auto-queuing creates fulfillment complexity."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' implies synchronous, authoritative data. In a distributed system with two bounded contexts, this is not achievable. The degree of acceptable staleness (seconds? minutes?) fundamentally shapes the architecture.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Synchronous API call to Context A on every page load — accurate but creates tight coupling and latency","(B) Eventually-consistent projection updated via domain events — decoupled but potentially stale by seconds to minutes","(C) Polling-based cache with configurable refresh interval"],"recommendation":"Option B (event-driven projection). Label the indicator as 'approximate' in the UI. This preserves bounded context independence while providing adequate visibility."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No specification for behavior when Context A (Warehouse) is unavailable. This is critical because the feature as described creates a hard dependency: if the warehouse system is down, can customers still place orders?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Orders fail when Context A is down — simple but high business risk","(B) Orders are accepted in 'pending validation' state — resilient but adds complexity","(C) Fall back to last-known stock projection — pragmatic but may allow overselling"],"recommendation":"Option B. Never let a backend system outage block revenue. Accept orders optimistically and validate asynchronously."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No mention of partial fulfillment or backorder scenarios. If an order has 5 lines and only 1 has insufficient stock, what happens to the other 4?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Operations","Finance"],"options":["(A) Block entire order","(B) Block only the insufficient line, fulfill the rest","(C) Accept full order, backorder insufficient items"],"recommendation":"This is a fundamental business policy decision that must come from the Sales/Operations leadership. Cannot be assumed."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The feature request as stated implies direct coupling between bounded contexts without an anti-corruption layer or integration pattern.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2 — Context B must never directly own or synchronously depend on Context A's StockLevel data."},{"violation":"The term 'real-time' stock indicator is misleading and architecturally dangerous. Implementing it literally would require synchronous cross-context calls that violate bounded context independence.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3 — Stock availability in Context B is an eventually-consistent projection, not authoritative real-time data."},{"violation":"'Automatically block' without a reservation pattern is a TOCTOU race condition that will inevitably violate the StockLevel >= 0 invariant under concurrent load.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-5 — StockLevel cannot go below zero, and stock-based blocking requires reservations, not point-in-time checks."}]}
